topLevel.body: I almost chose 'servicio' because it sounds more formal, but 'baño' felt more natural. Glad I stuck with my gut on this one, though it's good to know 'servicio' isn't wrong.

reply.body: Yeah, I went with 'servicio' initially thinking it was the more polite option for a formal setting, like a nice restaurant. But you're right, 'baño' is what I hear most often, even in places that feel pretty upscale.

topLevel.body: Took me a second to remember if it was 'pensar en' or 'pensar a'. I always get those mixed up with 'soñar con' sometimes. Glad I chose 'en'!

reply.body: Right? It's definitely 'pensar en' for ideas. 'Soñar con' is another one that trips me up. I think it's because the English uses 'dream about' but Spanish uses 'con'.

topLevel.body: Okay, I picked 'Espero el viaje con ansias'. I was a little confused because 'esperar' alone can mean 'to wait,' but the 'con ansias' part really makes it clear you're excited about it. I almost went with something involving 'tener ganas' but that felt a bit too informal for some contexts.

reply.body: I agree! The 'con ansias' is key. I also think 'Tengo muchas ganas de que llegue el viaje' is a great option, maybe even more common in everyday speech in some regions. It really depends on the vibe you're going for. Good pick!

topLevel.body: I almost went with 'conocer' for possession of information, specifically the 'know a fact' part. It felt so close to 'being familiar with' a fact. Glad I double-checked the explanation about 'saber' for specific data points.

reply.body: Totally! That's the trickiest part. I used to get them mixed up all the time, especially when thinking about knowing *about* something. 'Saber' for the direct info, 'conocer' for the relationship or familiarity seems to be the key.

topLevel.body: Okay, I went with 'vivo en Madrid'. It felt the most natural. I almost got tricked by thinking maybe 'resido en' was the more formal/permanent option, but thankfully I remembered the 'en' for location.

reply.body: Yeah, 'residir' definitely sounds more formal, more like official documentation. 'Vivir en' is what you'd use in everyday conversation when telling someone where your home is, even if it's permanent.

topLevel.body: I almost picked the imperfect tense for this one! It's so easy to confuse it with the simple present when talking about past habits. The 'cada día' example really drove home the present tense distinction for frequency. Good question!

reply.body: I hear you! The imperfect is definitely a trap for past habitual actions. I think the key here is that the question specifically asks about *current* frequency, not past. The simple present is the go-to for ongoing routines.

topLevel.body: I almost picked the option about a single, completed action. It's so easy to confuse the preterite and imperfect when talking about the past, especially with verbs like 'comer'. I'm glad I reread the distinction about ongoing vs. completed events.

reply.body: Totally! For me, it's the opposite. I tend to over-use the imperfect for everything in the past. I have to consciously remind myself that if it's a specific, finished event, the preterite is the way to go.

topLevel.body: I almost went with the option that used 'opinar', but 'parecer' is so much more common colloquially for this kind of thing. The impersonal construction is definitely the key here. Glad I saw this one, I’ve definitely used it wrong before without realizing!

reply.body: Totally agree! 'Opinar' feels a bit too formal for everyday chat, like you're really trying to elicit a deep thought. 'Parecer' is way smoother for quick opinions or suggestions. 'Qué te parece' is my go-to.

topLevel.body: I almost picked the preterite of 'ir'! It's so easy to fall into thinking of future actions as completed events sometimes. The 'ir a + infinitive' distinction for planned future is definitely what I need to keep drilling.

reply.body: Oh yeah, the preterite trap is real! I often have to pause and consciously remind myself if it's a planned future action versus something that already happened. 'Voy a estar allí' sounds so natural, but getting the grammatical structure right consistently is the goal!

topLevel.body: I almost picked the infinitive option out of habit! It's so common to default to 'Es importante estudiar' in everyday speech. The subjunctive always feels like a hurdle, but I'm slowly getting the hang of these impersonal expressions.

reply.body: Totally get that! I was tempted by the infinitive too. It really highlights how important context is for when to deploy the subjunctive. 'Es importante que' is a definite trigger for it.

topLevel.body: Ugh, I almost picked 'tengo' purely on instinct, but then I second-guessed myself because of all the irregular verbs. Glad I went with my gut though! Sometimes the most obvious answer IS the correct one, even with Spanish.

For a second there, I almost went with 'se les considera' thinking of the implied plural people doing the considering, but then remembered the subject is 'el mejor', which is singular. That 'se' can be tricky!

reply.body: I did the exact same thing at first glance! It's a classic trap. The key is truly focusing on the grammatical subject of 'considerar' and not getting sidetracked by who is doing the considering.

topLevel.body: Okay, I picked A, but I always second-guess myself with this stuff. Is it really that straightforward? I feel like I've heard it used differently sometimes, like with 'llevar' or 'tener'.

reply.body: Yeah, it can get tricky! The explanation is for the basic 'estar + gerundio' structure. You're right though, constructions like 'llevo dos horas estudiando' or 'tengo mucho trabajo acumulado' use the gerundio too, just in different contexts to show duration or a state resulting from an action.

topLevel.body: Ugh, I almost picked 'yendo'. I still get confused between the present tense and the present participle sometimes. 'Voy' makes sense though, thinking about it as 'I go to the store'.

reply.body: Totally! 'Yendo' is the gerund, used for progressive tenses like 'estoy yendo'. 'Voy' is the actual conjugated verb for 'I go'. Happens to the best of us!

topLevel.body: Okay, I think I got C, ' Vivo en Madrid.' It felt like the most straightforward use of 'en' for location. Sometimes these prepositions still get me, especially when thinking about origin vs. destination.

reply.body: Yes, 'Vivo en Madrid' is definitely correct! I sometimes get confused thinking about 'por' and 'para' when talking about movement towards a place, but for just stating where you are or where you live, 'en' feels pretty safe.

topLevel.body: I almost chose 'estar' because I was thinking about how a cold is a temporary state. But then I remembered 'tener dolor de cabeza' and that clarified it for me. 'Tener' feels more like you *have* the illness itself.

reply.body: That's a great way to think about it! The 'tener' examples are solid. I also find it helps to consider what you *would* say if it were temporary. 'Estoy resfriado' (I have a cold) is definitely common, but that's more about the current state of being sick, not possessing the illness itself as a condition.

topLevel.body: Definitely got tripped up by the subjunctive choice! I went with 'fuese' initially because it felt so hypothetical, but rereading the explanation, 'midiera' makes perfect sense for that 'wishing' context. Ojalá is such a useful word we don't have a direct equivalent for.

reply.body: I know, Ojalá is unique! I almost chose the conditional too, but then remembered the explanation always emphasizes the imperfect subjunctive for present unreal wishes. Glad I reviewed it, it's easy to mix those up.

topLevel.body: Chose 'Es evidente que...' This one felt pretty straightforward. I guess my brain is starting to internalize these 'ser' constructions. Still stumble on 'estar' for temporary states though.

reply.body: Yeah, 'Es evidente' is a great synonym for 'Es obvio'. Nice work! Don't worry about 'estar', that's a classic sticking point. Keep those daily exercises coming!

topLevel.body: I almost picked 'servicio' because I've heard it used in some very upscale hotels in Spain. But 'baño' just feels so much more natural for everyday use. Glad to see that confirmed!

reply.body: Yeah, 'servicio' can sound a bit dated or overly formal sometimes. 'Baño' is definitely the way to go unless you're aiming for that hyper-polite, almost archaic tone. Good to know the distinction!

topLevel.body: Okay, I almost picked 'a'. I always forget if 'pensar' uses 'en' or 'a' for ideas.  'Pienso en mi futuro' sounds more natural than 'Pienso a mi futuro', thankfully the explanation confirmed it.

reply.body: Yeah, that 'a' vs 'en' distinction with 'pensar' trips me up too! Sometimes it feels like it can be 'pensar en algo' (to reflect on) or 'pensar algo' (to think something up, like an idea). The 'en' is definitely for the reflective part.

topLevel.body: Ugh, I almost picked 'Espero el viaje'. 'Esperar' is so tricky! It really does mean wait for sometimes, not just hope. I ended up going with the 'con ansias' structure. Feels more natural for 'look forward to'.

reply.body: Totally understand! I also hesitated. The 'tengo ganas de' option was tempting, but 'con ansias' felt a bit stronger for specific anticipation like a trip. Good choice!

topLevel.body: Okay, I picked the 'information' option for 'saber'. It felt intuitive because I *know* facts. But the 'familiarity' for 'conocer' tripped me up a tiny bit. Do you guys use 'conocer' for like, 'I know this app' meaning I'm familiar with its features? Makes sense, but still a little sticky.

reply.body: Totally! I think 'conocer' definitely applies to knowing apps or general concepts really well, like you're familiar with how they work. It’s more about deep familiarity than just having a specific piece of data. Glad someone else found that part tricky too!

topLevel.body: I almost picked 'residir en' out of habit from reading formal texts, but then I remembered the question specifically asked about 'permanencia' and 'vivir en' feels more like the everyday statement of fact. It's subtle!

reply.body: Yes, 'residir' definitely has a more formal, official ring to it. 'Vivir en' is what you'd use when introducing yourself or telling a friend. Good point about the nuance!

topLevel.body: I picked the wrong option at first! I was thinking about using the progressive tense for 'continuing,' but the explanation makes sense about the simple present for things that just happen regularly. 'Leo el periódico' sounds so much more natural than 'Estoy leyendo el periódico todos los días,' which feels like I'm in the middle of reading it right this second.

reply.body: I agree! The progressive really implies an ongoing action *at this moment*. It's a common confusion point. Spanish often uses the simple present where English might use 'always' or 'every day' with the progressive, but here it's clear the simple present is the key for habitual actions.

topLevel.body: I picked the imperfect because it seemed to fit the 'habitual' part of the question so well. It's like painting a scene of the past rather than marking a single event. Makes sense that it's used for ongoing or repeated actions.

reply.body: Totally agree! The imperfect feels more descriptive, painting the picture. The preterite would feel more like checking off a box for a completed action, which isn't what they were asking about here.
